query: vitamin A deficiency and tooth formation effects

retrieved_docs.title: Diet and Nutrition to Prevent Dental Problems -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Besides dental caries, nutrition may contribute to developing other dental and oral mucosa pathologies. Delayed tooth eruption and salivary gland dysfunction may be linked to protein deficiencies. Vitamin A deficiency leads to impaired epithelial tissue development, tooth formation, and enamel hypoplasia. Vitamin D and calcium deficiency cause hypo-mineralization, delayed eruption, the absence of lamina dura, and abnormal alveolar bone patterns.

retrieved_docs.title: Diet and Nutrition to Prevent Dental Problems -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Delayed wound healing, dentin malformations, bleeding gums, and defective collagen formation may result from vitamin C deficiency. Angular cheilosis and periodontal diseases are associated with a lack of vitamin B. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin A -- Adverse Effects -- Vitamin A Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A is highly teratogenic if administered during pregnancy. Retinoids affect the expression of the homeobox gene Hoxb-1 , which regulates the axial patterning of the embryo. Birth abnormalities include craniofacial, cardiac, and central nervous system malformations. Therefore, treatment with vitamin A should be avoided in pregnant patients, except in areas where vitamin A deficiency is prevalent. In such cases, vitamin A supplementation should not exceed 10,000 IU daily. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin A Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A is critical for regulating gene expression and guiding cell differentiation during embryonic development. The embryo is particularly susceptible to the teratogenic effects of excessive amounts of vitamin A during the first trimester, which is a phase marked by rapid organogenesis. Teratogenic effects are primarily associated with high doses of retinoid medications such as isotretinoin rather than dietary sources of vitamin A. A minimum safe dose of oral retinoids during pregnancy has not been established. Isotretinoin is estimated to increase the risk of fetal malformations by 25-fold. [5] The mechanism is believed to be linked to a toxic effect on neural crest cells, possibly affecting the regulation of axial patterning in the embryo via the expression of the homeobox gene Hoxb-1 . [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin A -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the physiological roles of vitamin A, including its impact on vision, immune function, cellular differentiation, and gene regulation. Screen patients for vitamin A deficiency or toxicity using appropriate clinical assessments and laboratory testing. Apply best practices for managing vitamin A–related conditions, including deficiency-related ocular manifestations and hypervitaminosis A complications. Collaborate with the interprofessional healthcare team to optimize care for patients receiving vitamin A supplementation and ensure their efficacy and safety. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin A Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnant women should refrain from using oral retinoid medications and excessive vitamin A supplements to mitigate the risk of vitamin A-related malformations. They should consult healthcare professionals and prenatal care providers to ensure proper prenatal nutrition and avoid drugs or supplements that are contraindicated during pregnancy. Prescribers should only recommend oral retinoids to women of childbearing age who are not pregnant and use reliable birth control methods to prevent the potential risk of vitamin A-related teratogenic effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin A Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the metabolism of Vitamin A in the body. Identify common manifestations of Vitamin A deficiency. Identify populations at risk for Vitamin A deficiency. Collaborate with the interprofessional team to improve patient care. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Nutrition: Micronutrient Intake, Imbalances, and Interventions -- Issues of Concern -- Fat-Soluble Vitamins
retrieved_docs.content: While significantly less frequent, excess intake of vitamin A can lead to hypervitaminosis, which can manifest with hypercalcemia, congenital disabilities (teratogenicity), bone/joint pain, cheilitis, stomatitis, conjunctivitis, desquamation, alopecia, headache, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, fever, and liver dysfunction. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Delayed Eruption
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic and prolonged malnutrition, often associated with vitamin deficiencies during childhood, is another factor that also correlates with delayed eruption. [2] Moreover, various syndromes have been identified as causing delayed eruption, including Down syndrome, Gardner syndrome, cleidocranial dysostosis, anhidrotic ectodermal dysplasia, Hutchinson-Gilford syndrome, Bloch–Sulzberger syndrome, Apert syndrome, and Axenfeld–Rieger syndrome. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Nutrition is also of specific concern in the development and growth of teeth. The availability of calcium and phosphate in the diet and the amount of vitamin D in the body are all factors that can lead to changes in the enamel and growth of healthy, normal teeth. Defective calcification occurs when these factors are deficient, leading to abnormal tooth development throughout life. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Teratogenic Medications -- Issues of Concern -- Vitamin A
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A, in large doses, can also be teratogenic. A pregnant woman may receive excessive vitamin A by eating excess vitamin A-rich food or taking nutritional supplements with vitamin A or drugs containing retinoids. [30] Both the excess and the lack of vitamin A can cause embryonic malformations. Retinoic acid is essential for early embryogenesis and, subsequently, for the maturation and development of tissues and organs. High doses of vitamin A in pregnant rats caused neural tube defects, for instance, exencephaly, spina bifida with meningocele, hydrocephalus, eye malformations, and cleft palate. [31] In humans, high doses of vitamin A can also induce thymic and cardiovascular abnormalities such as hypoplastic aorta and cardiovascular transposition. The pharmacologic action is exerted on the cranial neural crest cells and an unknown central nervous system cellular group. The carboxylate group and the side chain of the molecule give the retinoids their teratogenic potency. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin A Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The teratogenic effects of vitamin A intake were first discovered in animal studies during the 1950s. Subsequently, extensive research has been conducted to elucidate the underlying causes of the effects. [22] Excessive vitamin A consumption during pregnancy has been associated with various congenital malformations in humans. The disabilities associated with this condition include abnormalities in the central nervous system, such as microcephaly and hydrocephalus; cardiac issues, such as transposition of the great vessels; craniofacial deformities, such as cleft lip and palate; limb abnormalities; and urinary tract disorders. [5] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin A -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A is essential for mucosal epithelial regeneration and supports the function of neutrophils, macrophages, and natural killer cells, making it crucial for innate immunity. This vitamin is also crucial in cell morphogenesis, differentiation, and proliferation, as well as for gene regulation. Furthermore, as an antioxidant, vitamin A helps reduce free radical damage to DNA. A deficiency in vitamin A is believed to contribute to neoplastic transformation and carcinogenesis. [5]

Vitamin A provides retinoid‑mediated vision and epithelial health; niacin delivers B‑vitamin‑mediated metabolic and neuro‑protective effects; calcium pantothenate drives co‑enzyme A‑dependent metabolism through its phosphate‑chelating activity. Their combined actions enhance antioxidant capacity, immune function and growth, contributing to comprehensive patient care. Clinicians should note that calcium pantothenate is contraindicated in individuals with a history of nephrolithiasis (kidney stones), and products containing this compound should be avoided in such patients.

In multivitamin formulations vitamin D works synergistically with calcium, iron, vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin E, the full B‑vitamin complex, and trace minerals to provide complementary nutritional benefits: calcium pantothenate supports cellular metabolism within the B‑vitamin spectrum, while vitamin D promotes skeletal health. Collectively, these nutrients—vitamin A (various chemical forms), niacin (vitamin B3), calcium pantothenate (vitamin B5) and vitamin D—are extensively utilized as active or inactive ingredients across oral multivitamin‑mineral products, prenatal supplements, DHA‑enriched blends, iron‑fortified formulations and topical dermatologic preparations. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 30
retrieved_docs.content: preparation, Multivitamin + mineral preparations, Multivitamins with minerals, Multivitamin agent, and the generic “Multivitamins” class. These formulations are frequently combined with minerals to address نقص التغذية and often contain vitamin K, vitamin A (retinoids), and vitamin C alongside the B‑vitamin complex. Antianemic preparations are classified both as iron‑based antianemic agents and as formulations that contain vitamin B₁₂ (cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin, hydroxocobalamin) together with folic acid, reflecting their dual function in correcting iron‑deficiency anemia and anemia caused by B‑vitamin deficiencies. Subcategories include iron bivalent oral agents, iron + folic‑acid combinations, and iron in other combinations. The IRON/MULTIVITAMINS family unites iron‑containing multivitamin products under a single umbrella.
